hazard.summary: Model-scoped token exhaustion (429) is misclassified as provider-level billing failure, causing auth-profile cooldown and skipping same-provider fallbacks.
signals: Claude Max plan has separate per-model token pools (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku)
Opus token pool exhausted → treated as billing-disabled
falls through to weaker local model; user sees degraded loop/no tool access
Temporary limit (429 / Retry-After) treated as billing failure

constraints: Temporary limits (429 / token pool exhaustion / Retry-After) must never mark a provider/auth-profile as billing-disabled.
Cooldown must be correctly scoped: model-scoped exhaustion may cool down only that model, while allowing same-provider fallbacks on the same credential.
Provider/auth-profile cooldown must be distinct from billing-disabled and only triggered by true billing/credential failure signals.
verification: Configure primary=anthropic/claude-opus-4-6 and fallbacks=[anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, ...] on a Max plan; induce Opus pool exhaustion (429).
Before fix: observe auth profile enters billing-disabled/cooldown and Sonnet is skipped.
After fix: on 429/token exhaustion, advance to Sonnet using same auth profile; do not mark billing-disabled; ensure successful response from fallback.
Add an automated test that simulates a model-scoped 429/token-exhaustion error and asserts: decision=advance_model and cooldown applies only to the exhausted model.
notes: This is a classic error classification + cooldown scoping bug: model-level temporary limits are being escalated to provider/profile billing failure, producing silent total failover degradation.
